text: This is how you're going. I guess I'll go over here. We've got waters, too. Great. All right. Yeah, hi, everyone. Thanks, Tushar. My name's Teddy Collins. I'm the EVP of finance and happy long-time Team Ohana customer. And so we've got two great CFOs here with kind of long-storied histories across operations and other verticals. First, we've got Ben Gamble, who's the CFO and president of Brex. And he kind of came in, kind of headed operations, then took over finance, then the go-to-market motion for tens of thousands of customers. So great to see there. And then before that, led operations at SoFi, and then various kind of investment positions, starting with Goldman Sachs. And similar theme, Martin also kind of got his start in the investment area and private equity, and then the CFO at Funbox, which provided working capital solutions for small businesses, and most recently and currently CFO at Vercel provides developer tools for folks to build and deploy and scale high-performance AI apps. So thank you for joining. Thank you for having us. Thank you. And I just kind of want to get started. Both your companies are AI native, so kind of on the cutting edge of AI. And given your background in operations and finance, how does that kind of shape your vision on what the purpose, what AI can do for your company and specifically within the Office of Finance? Ben, I'd love for you to kick that off for us. Sure, happy to. Well, firstly, unlike Martin, I don't have the luxury of being an AI native company, but we're going through that transformational shift as a company to become one. So we were started in 2017 by Contex, so existed far beyond, far before, I should say, this recent AI wave. As we think about finance, I think that, you know, historically, finance has been quite a reactive function and role where we're closing the books after the month closes or we're chasing receipts of payments that have already gone out. And I think with what we applied to at Brex from like 2018 onwards was trying to create a proactive motion within finance. So how do we embed sort of intelligence into workflows that finance professionals engage with whether it expenses cars payment flows in general so that you can be a bit more on the front foot I think AI for us is taking us a step further than that which is like, how do you actually make it more predictive? So how can AI use the context, or the agents use the context in which it operates around your expense policy, how you think about workflows, to actually be able to proactively and predictively sort of resolve issues before they even become issues? So an example of this is we have an audit agent whereby that agent can go and summarize all the expenses for a company, can actually proactively suggest these are the repayments that should exist because they fall outside of your expense policy, and even take a step further of actually automating and actually taking action on that workflow. So you don't actually need a finance person in the loop for the sort of more mundane and execution-oriented aspects of the work. So that's how we think about it, Rex. Yes, we don't sell into CFOs. We sell most into developers. So AI and the finance org at Vercel has been most around improving internal workflows. So just a couple of examples. We have like an ASC 606 revenue recognition tool that we just built ourselves. You can take any contract or any bulk upload a contract, plug it in there, and you sort of get a highlight of which are the contracts that have like some very special terms that you should make sure you address individually. And here are all the contracts that are like super boilerplate straight forward and can just be recognized pro rata. So that's like one tool that we have. We use it for BVA, so we connect into NetSuite and Stripe data. It's kind of like a B- quality, candidly. But it's pretty fast, right? So you can ask a quick question like, hey, what happened in this? What's the change in spend from this vendor to this vendor, month of a month, a quarter of a quarter, whatever? And you can get an answer really, really fast. So it helps sort of speed up the process and make data more accessible sort of at the fingertips. In the past, we're pretty dependent on help from our data team to run advanced SQL queries. So the data team actually built this. It's called internally D0. We have a tool called V0, so the D is for data, where you can now just write pretty complex SQL queries using an LLM and have that be run straight into our Snowflake database, So you're much less dependent on a sort of help from the data team to run more complex requests and workflows. So for us it just been making every individual in the team more efficient ability to self more And as a result I think we need slightly fewer people But we pretty far from I think the stage where like you're no longer getting anyone in finance and you can just have AI, like, close the books for you and do everything. I think that will take some time. Of course, bias. But huge sort of efficiency improvements. So you might be able to do it with a slightly leaner team than in the past. Yeah, I know. Martin, you said you don't kind of sell these applications outside of Vercel, but from everything you've described so far, I think you kind of maybe owe it to the world to release those. I think there are actually some of them as case studies on our website. So you can go and download them there. They're free open source. But yeah, some of these other ones, I mean, you can just make them yourself. Like the ASC606 one was like, I actually built the first version of it in prompting plain English language. It's going to sound like a huge sell for Vercel. but I literally went to V0, which is a give-for-sell product. I typed in what I wanted. I got a draft. It looked how I thought it would look, but then when you uploaded the contract, that output was not how I wanted it. So then I just asked it to, and I needed a couple of attempts because maybe I wasn't too crisp on exactly what I wanted. But then I did that, and now it spits out sort of the results the way I want it. But the revenue team on my accounting side, they actually built their own because they have a slightly different preference for how they want to have that information. And they just do it themselves. And I think that's, like, the key. Unlike in the past, even if you wanted to build some internal tools, you had to get engineering resources and kind of difficult, especially working at a tech company where, like, you want all your engineers working on stuff that's going to move the business forward, asking them to build some, you know, revenue recognition tool for you. It's probably not going to be super high priority. Whereas now you can just do it yourself and get pretty decent outputs. Yeah, and I want to kind of stay on that topic for one second. You've already kind of described kind of going from zero to one, and kind of a lot of people have kind of built apps, experimented, you know, got these different unlocks. But how do you take those apps and kind of bring them to scale on, hey, that might work for you, but like the 50 or 100 different applications that are kind of all sprawling about, what kind of happens internally to validate and to use and to poke and to improve those applications. Yes I think we definitely have a surge in what we internally call small software which is basically almost like individual use software And that sort of meant to improve the efficiency of any one individual Then we do have some tools that are a little bit more centrally run. And then we have, you know, real sort of, you know, Okta security and that kind of stuff, making sure we're not sort of leaking information. And the difference between those two is like, do they have access to sort of underlying information. So like if there's a connection to say like a Snowflake database or NetSuite or, you know, Greenhouse or Team Ohana data or something that is more sensitive, then we tend to lock it down. If it is, you put in an Excel sheet and you get it some, perhaps there's some outputs from it, but you're not feeding sort of live information. We have much lower sort of security requirements for those types of instances. But boy, do we have a surge in tools that people use. Even like a personal example, I haven't built this yet, but all the people on my team have. I've never found a good to-do list that I like. I've tried everything, but I still sort of come back to pen and pencil. But I have all the people on my team that have sort of built their own ideal version of a to-do list that fits their workflow as opposed to having to try to find some third-party tool that someone else decided on the workflow and you're now adapting. to that. And that's obviously super easy to do. It's like a database of sorts. And yeah, you can have as many of those as you want in an organization. Yeah. I mean, I'd love to geek out about productivity tools, which is probably literally my favorite topic. But I want to get back over to Ben. And you serve tens of thousands of offices of finance. But I also know you're kind of a big proponent of investing and bringing early-stage companies into your finance stack. Can you tell me a bit more about your approach to your CFO finance stack? Yeah, I think that a big part of this is really just trying to meet customers where they are. I think that there's a tension at play with any company that is providing an AI tool or platform externally to their customers about recognizing the audience you're speaking to. I think when we think about our startup customers, smaller customers, they're more technologically enabled, they're more technologically fluent, They will probably, especially if they've started within the last year or so, be sort of AI native. And so their propensity to adopt and really run with more sort of AI forward features and functionality is like just much, much higher.
